result:
There’s a small pond behind the house where I grew up, a shallow bowl of rainwater ringed by cattails and poison ivy. When I was seven I believed it was an ocean that hadn’t decided what it wanted to be yet. I could sit cross-legged on the splintered dock for whole August afternoons, staring at the same clot of algae until it turned into an island, then a continent, then a green star in a galaxy of tadpoles. I was waiting for something to rise—maybe the single goldfish that had allegedly been dumped there by a neighbor kid the year I was born. Rumor said it had grown monstrous on mosquito larvae and the eggs of drowned birds. Rumor said it glowed.

Nothing ever surfaced except plastic bait and the occasional lost baseball. Still, I kept returning, convinced that if I watched long enough the water would forget I was there and reveal its real life. I practiced breathing through my mouth so the ripples of my exhales wouldn’t betray me. I learned to keep my blinks short, like camera shutters, so I wouldn’t miss anything. One afternoon a thunderstorm pinned itself against the sky and the pond suddenly became a sheet of hammered pewter. For one flicker I saw it: a bright muscle flipping beneath the metal skin, a stroke of living color so undeniable I gasped. Then rain erased the image, if it had ever existed, and I ran home soaked and electric, certain I’d glimpsed the edge of a secret I was meant to guard.

I never told anyone. Secrets, I understood even then, are the only real estate children own outright. Over the decades the pond shrank—phragmites marched in, the town drained it twice for mosquito abatement, someone’s riding mower slid down the bank and leaked a rainbow—until it was little more than a boggy dent in the subdivision’s collective memory. But I carried that flash of gold like a coin sewn into a coat lining. On airplanes, during bad love affairs, in hospital waiting rooms, I would finger it absently, feeling its weight, its impossible warmth.

Last week I drove back for the first time in twenty years. The house is vinyl-sided now, painted a cheerful hostage beige. The dock has been replaced by a reinforced concrete retaining wall stamped to look like stone. A HOA sign warns against feeding wildlife and swimming “due to improved water quality.” I stood at the mandated setback distance and stared at the restored pond: a perfect oval, fountain in the center aerating the surface like a bartender shaking gin. No algae, no tadpoles, no rumors allowed. The water was so clear I could see the drain grate at the bottom, a black mandala of engineered safety.

I waited ten polite minutes, breathing through my nose. Nothing moved. I realized I had come to return the coin—to let the goldfish have its legend back—but the pond had already spent it on respectability. On the drive out I passed a kid balanced on a bike, eyes narrowed at the water with the same feral attention I remembered. For a second I wanted to pull over and tell him: blink fast, breathe slow, it’s still in there somewhere. Instead I kept going, because maybe the real gift isn’t the glimpse, it’s the hunt—decades of scanning surfaces, half-believing something luminous is practicing the same patience on you.

Tonight I’m miles away, city windows flickering like faulty lighthouses. I close my eyes and the pond reinstalls itself, deeper than the original, bordered now by memory’s permissive wild. I drop a line into the dark. I still haven’t decided which of us is the hook and which is the shine.
